candidate_problems.text: How can we formalize and measure the conjecture’s core condition—'prediction error cannot be driven low by shallow correlations alone'—in a way that distinguishes domains where invariant-tracking is structurally necessary from domains where shortcut loops suffice? The open tension is that the conjecture is intuitively compelling but currently underspecified: without operational criteria for shallow correlations, latent invariants, and dominant error-correcting pathways, it is hard to test, falsify, or compare across datasets and architectures.

What is the relative role of objective versus architecture and optimization in forcing latent-state learning? The conjecture attributes success mainly to domain structure and bottleneck mismatch, but there is an unresolved tension over whether next-token prediction alone is ever sufficient, or whether recurrence length, attention, memory, regularization, curriculum, and optimization dynamics are equally decisive in preserving the feedback loop needed to build reusable latent variables.

When models appear to learn reusable internal structure under next-token prediction, how can we tell whether those representations track real domain invariants rather than merely highly effective dataset-specific compressions? This is an open question about identifiability and robustness: predictive success, even long-range success, may still be compatible with non-causal or non-portable abstractions, so the conjecture raises but does not resolve what empirical tests would separate genuine invariant discovery from sophisticated shortcut learning.
